whatItDoes: Benchmark JavaScript code execution time by running a code snippet N times (configurable from 1 to 10,000 iterations). Reports total time, average time, min/max time, and operations per second using performance.now() for high-precision timing.
whyItExists: Comparing the performance of different code approaches requires accurate timing measurement; this tool provides a quick way to benchmark code snippets without setting up a full test framework.
whoShouldUse: JavaScript developers optimizing performance-critical code, educators teaching algorithm complexity, and anyone who needs to measure code execution time accurately.
useCases: Compare the performance of two different sorting algorithms
Measure the overhead of different JavaScript patterns (map vs for-loop)
Benchmark regex patterns for performance optimization
Test the speed of different JSON serialization approaches
Measure the cost of function calls vs inline code
Quick A/B testing of code implementations
instructions: Write or paste JavaScript code in the function body textarea
Use 'return' to return a value from the benchmarked code
Select the number of iterations (1, 10, 100, 1K, or 10K)
Click Run Benchmark to execute the code the specified number of times
Review the results: total time, average, min, max, and ops/sec
Click a quick example button to load a pre-written benchmark
examples: Array.sort vs manual sort algorithm on a 1000-element array
String concatenation with += vs array join
Math.sqrt vs manual square root approximation
bestPractices: Run benchmarks multiple times to account for browser warm-up and JIT optimization
Use a high iteration count (1000+) for more stable and reliable results
Avoid including setup/teardown code inside the benchmarked function
Be aware that browser tab throttling can affect timing accuracy
Compare relative performance rather than absolute numbers across machines
commonMistakes: Running too few iterations (1-10) which gives unreliable results due to JIT warm-up
Including console.log or DOM operations inside the benchmarked code
Comparing absolute times across different machines or browsers
Not accounting for garbage collection pauses in longer benchmarks
Assuming browser optimizations won't affect the benchmark (e.g., dead code elimination)
faq: Why are my results inconsistent between runs? — Browser JIT compilation optimizes code over time. Run multiple benchmarks and look at the average for stable results.
Is this safe to run arbitrary code? — The code runs in the browser sandbox via the Function constructor. It cannot access files or system resources, but it can consume CPU and memory.
What does ops/sec mean? — Operations per second is the inverse of average time: 1000 / avgTime(ms). Higher is better.
Can I benchmark async code? — This tool benchmarks synchronous code only. For async benchmarks, you would need a custom setup with await.
